text: A right extended posterolateral thoracotomy incision is performed, and the prior incision is incorporated into the thoracotomy.
The latissimus muscle is divided using electrocautery, and the serratus muscle is divided as well.
The sixth rib is removed, and the extrapleural dissection is performed.
Several ribs below and several ribs above are dissected extrapleurally to facilitate the placement of two finishetto retractors.
Superiorly, the dissection is taken to the subclavian vessels and the superior vena cava.
However, this is difficult to show in this video.

Once the lateral portion is dissected, the diaphragm is then dissected off the anterior portion of the chest wall using electrocautery, and the underlying peritoneum is dissected using a sponge stick.
The liver can be seen below the peritoneum.
Using electrocautery, the pericardium is entered.
and the diaphragm is removed circumferentially, maintaining the integrity of the pleural envelope.
The right atrium can be seen beating within the pericardial space.
The pericardium is then dissected superiorly using electrocautery.
Care is taken not to injure the superior vena cava below and the dissection is carried superiorly to the takeoff of the azagus vein.
The right main stem bronchus and pulmonary artery can be seen here.
Dissection of the level 10 and level 4 lymph nodes can help facilitate transection of the pulmonary artery as well.
Once this has been cleared, the right main pulmonary artery is encircled using a harking clamp.
And a red rubber catheter is placed behind the artery to facilitate placement of a stapler for subsequent transection of the artery.
And here the artery is transected with the stapler.
Then the superior pulmonary vein is encircled and is transected with the stapler.
This will provide exposure of the inferior pulmonary vein.
A red rubber catheter is again used to facilitate placement of the stapler.
With large bulky tumors, this anatomy may be distorted.
And it is very important to identify the four venous structures on the right.
The superior vena cava, superior pulmonary vein, inferior pulmonary vein, and inferior vena cava prior to transection of any of the structures.
Now you can see the superior and inferior pulmonary veins have been transected.
Now, below the diaphragm, the inferior phrenic vein is identified and clipped.
Avulsion of this vein from the inferior vena cava can result in profuse hemorrhage, and therefore it is important to identify it and transect it prior to proceeding with the dissection.

Once that vein has been transected, the cruce of the diaphragm is dissected, care is taken to identify the esophagus, and then the posterior pericardium is taken superiorly until the bronchus is identified.
Then the bronchus is encircled and transected using a TA30 stapling device.
After the bronchus is transected using a knife, the specimen is delivered from the chest.
The specimen can be seen with the pleura, diaphragm intact, and the tumor in the middle of this area.
A pericardial fat pad is placed on the bronchial stump.
Here, the fat pad is placed medial to the vena cava.
On other occasions, the fat pad may be placed lateral to the vena cava.
The argon beam electrocoagulator is then used to obtain hemostasis.
Several stace sutures are placed here in the posterior pericardium.
And then a Gore-Tex patch is placed in a running fashion to the inferior edge of the pericardium.
Then approximately six to eight sutures are placed circumferentially around the ribs.
to anchor the diaphragmatic patch in place.
The diaphragmatic patch is fashioned to fit the lower portion of the hemithorax.
Then using the stay sutures, the posterior portion of the pericardium is sutured to
a Dexon mesh to prevent cardiac herniation.
This is performed in a continuous fashion, both anteriorly and posteriorly.
And inferiorly, this is sutured to the Gore-Tex patch.
Please note that the Dexon mesh is already fenestrated.
However, if GORE-TEX is used for the pericardial reconstruction, fenestration must be performed to prevent cardiac tamponade.
Here, the Dexon mesh is seen completely covering the heart.
The diaphragm is keeping the intestinal contents within the belly.
and the pericardial fat pad is secured on the bronchial stump.
A chest tube is placed and the incision is closed in the usual fashion.
